medications.indication_rationale: First-line topical therapy for acute diffuse bacterial otitis externa: the antibiotic treats the canal infection at high local concentration and the corticosteroid reduces canal oedema and pain. A quinolone combination is preferred where the tympanic membrane cannot be visualised because it avoids aminoglycoside ototoxicity risk if the drum is perforated. The AI Doctor cannot prescribe directly and must arrange this through a GP or telehealth prescriber promptly.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 3 drops to the affected (right) ear twice daily for 7 days
contraindications_in_this_case: known hypersensitivity to quinolones or corticosteroids

medications.indication_rationale: Add a regular NSAID to the existing paracetamol for multimodal analgesia — the pain is severe and paracetamol alone is insufficient. Combined paracetamol + ibuprofen is more effective than either alone.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400 mg orally every 8 hours as needed with food

medications.indication_rationale: Continue regular paracetamol as the analgesic backbone alongside ibuprofen, ensuring the dose stays within the maximum of 4 g in 24 hours (the patient has already taken about 3 g).
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000 mg orally every 4-6 hours, maximum 4 g in 24 hours

medications.indication_rationale: Systemic antibiotics are NOT indicated for uncomplicated acute otitis externa — topical therapy achieves far higher local concentrations. Oral antibiotics are reserved for infection spreading beyond the canal (cellulitis), immunocompromise, or necrotising otitis externa, none of which are present here.

medications.drug_name: cotton-bud aural cleaning / canal instrumentation by the patient
medications.indication_rationale: Cotton buds and self-instrumentation traumatise the canal skin, reintroduce bacteria and worsen otitis externa — this must be explicitly advised against, not recommended.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Instil the ear drops on a strict twice-daily schedule and lie on the unaffected side for about 5 minutes afterwards so the drops penetrate deeply; keep the medication bottle by the bedside as a visual cue.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Consistent, correctly-administered dosing settles canal inflammation faster and improves cure rates.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the ear completely dry — avoid swimming and water entry, and when showering place a cotton ball coated in petroleum jelly at the outer ear.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Moisture perpetuates canal maceration and bacterial growth; a dry canal is essential for healing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not insert cotton buds or any object into the ear canal.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Avoiding canal trauma allows the epithelium to heal and prevents mechanical reintroduction of bacteria.

patient_education_points.point: The prescribed ear drops combine an antibiotic to clear the infection with a steroid to bring down the swelling and pain inside the ear canal.
Symptoms should start improving within 48-72 hours of starting the drops; complete the full course (usually 7-10 days) even once you feel better, and don't stop early.
Keep the ear dry and never use cotton buds — water and canal trauma are the main things that keep otitis externa going.
This diagnosis is presumptive over telehealth and depends on your response to treatment — see your GP if it is not improving in 2-3 days, and seek urgent care if any of the warning signs appear.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review in 48-72 hours if there is no symptomatic improvement (sooner if worsening)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: pain control, discharge, canal oedema and response to topical therapy; consider an ear swab and aural toilet if refractory, and reassess for any red-flag features
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: spreading redness or swelling behind the ear, facial weakness, high fever, uncontrolled pain, or any new neurological symptoms

must_include_items: Recognise an external ear canal infection from the history (itch prodrome, purulent otorrhoea, pain on ear movement)
Arrange prompt prescription topical antibiotic/steroid ear drops via a GP or telehealth prescriber
Optimise analgesia by adding a regular NSAID to the existing paracetamol (within safe limits)
Give ear-care advice: keep the ear dry and do not use cotton buds
Safety-net for the necrotising/mastoiditis red flags and advise GP review in 48-72 hours if not improving
acceptable_alternatives: A telehealth prescriber issuing the topical drops directly rather than referral to a GP
Acetic-acid or antibiotic-only ear drops as an alternative topical agent
Advising a same-day in-person review specifically to obtain the prescription and aural toilet, provided it is framed as prompt rather than emergency
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange prescription topical therapy (treating with analgesia/keep-dry only)
Failing to optimise analgesia for severe pain
Failing to give keep-dry / no-cotton-bud advice
Failing to safety-net for necrotising otitis externa / mastoiditis
errors_of_commission: Prescribing or recommending oral/systemic antibiotics for uncomplicated otitis externa
Recommending cotton-bud or self-instrumentation cleaning of the canal
Over-triaging to the emergency department or 000 for a localised external ear infection
Reassuring with pure self-care and no prescription or safety-net
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising otitis externa, arranging topical antibiotic/steroid therapy, optimising analgesia, and safety-netting. Neither over-triage to ED/000 nor pure self-care without prescription is a passing answer.
